exemplar.title: Do the ever-improving tools of an area of knowledge always result in improved knowledge? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: Do the ever-improving tools of an area of knowledge always result in improved knowledge? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained and clear focus on the prescribed title throughout the essay
Effective and coherent linkage to two Areas of Knowledge with well-chosen examples (MRI vs radium, BCG matrix vs Taylorâs theory)
Logical sequencing of arguments and recognition of alternative perspectives
Good integration of argument and counterâargument to show evaluative engagement
weaknesses: Depth of analysis is uneven, with key epistemic concepts (reliability, validity, methodological constraints) only briefly addressed
Some factual inaccuracies (e.g., conflation of radium and x-ray technology) undermine precision
Evaluation of counter-arguments lacks fuller exploration of implications to reach highest bands
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessment places this essay squarely in the 7â8 band: it maintains a sustained, coherent focus on the title, makes clear links to areas of knowledge, and uses specific examples effectively, while showing awareness of different points of view. However, occasional superficial treatment of core epistemic concepts and factual imprecision prevent it from achieving the sustained depth and nuance characteristic of the 9â10 band. In reverse grading, to match the examinerâs overall mark of 8, the essayâs clear strengths outweigh its weaknesses but do not fully justify the top band. Therefore, a final score of 8 accurately reflects its quality.

annotations.comment: The phrase âimproved knowledgeâ is repeated frequently, creating stylistic monotony. Varying terminology would improve readability and impact.
The phrase âradiological carâ is ambiguous and distracts from the argument. The student should clarify or correct this terminology to maintain coherence.
The critique of Taylorâs theory acknowledges multiple perspectives and ethical issues, demonstrating balanced evaluation and awareness of context in human science.
The claim that overworking workers is less efficient than operational optimisation needs empirical support. Adding data or case studies would substantiate this critique.
The term âlababoritalâ appears to be a misspelling of âlaboratorial.â Precise terminology ensures clarity, so proofreading technical terms is essential.
The final sentence of the main body is abrupt and does not fully resolve the discussion. A more explicit final evaluation would provide a stronger argumentative closure.
The essay clearly explains the structure and purpose of the BCG matrix, effectively linking its data-driven approach to decision making in business management.

The synthesis of natural and human science examples is coherent, but the conclusion does not clearly articulate the overarching implications for the knowledge question. Clarify how both cases inform the central claim.
The expansion of the BCG matrix discussion demonstrates a solid understanding of its application and differentiation from other business tools, showing strong subject knowledge.
The student correctly restates the prescribed title in full, ensuring a clear focus on the knowledge question throughout the essay.
The student clearly identifies the two areas of knowledgeâNatural science and Human scienceâeffectively framing the scope for subsequent analysis.

The introduction provides broad definitions of âknowledgeâ and âimprovingâ but remains largely descriptive. The student should focus these definitions into a concise knowledge question to sharpen the essayâs analytical focus.

The thesis statement lacks specificity in articulating how ever-improving tools may both advance and impede knowledge. Refining the claim to signal evaluative judgments would strengthen the central argument.

While the MRI example is appropriate, the analysis stops short of examining key epistemic concepts such as reliability or validity. The student should explicitly connect the example to these concepts to deepen the evaluation.

The explanation of the BCG matrix remains descriptive. To fulfil a TOK essayâs evaluative requirement, discuss how the toolâs underlying assumptions affect the validity and scope of business knowledge.
The transition sentence âThus, this essay will exploreâ¦â is redundant after the introduction. A more seamless move into the first AOK discussion would maintain argumentative momentum.
The essay conflates the historical development of NMR and MRI without clear distinction. Providing accurate chronological context and technical differences would improve precision.

The claim that radium âunlockedâ the use of x-rays is historically inaccurate. RÃ¶ntgen discovered x-rays before Curieâs work on radioactivity. Ensuring factual accuracy is crucial for authoritative analysis.
The description of MRI origins lacks precise dates or landmark studies. Incorporating specific research milestones would strengthen the credibility and depth of the historical narrative.

The discussion of the Radium Girls case effectively illustrates the unintended consequences of technological tools and engages with ethical dimensions, demonstrating awareness of broader implications.
The analysis of the BCG matrix lacks a clear connection back to the central question of whether tools invariably improve knowledge. Reinforcing this link would enhance coherence.
The introduction to Taylorâs scientific management should include discussion of its methodological foundations and research context to clarify how the tool generates knowledge about motivation.
Citations such as â(BCG Classics Revisited: The Growth Share Matrix, 2014)â are inconsistently formatted. Adhering to one citation style improves professionalism and readability.

Listing alternative management styles without analysing how they challenge Taylorâs methodology limits the depth of evaluation. A deeper exploration of these implications would strengthen the argument.
The closing statement remains too general and lacks specificity. The student should more clearly articulate how evidence and utility shape the production of knowledge.

described by levels
1â5 or is not a

on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
